paragraphs.text: "When someone is pained over the prevailing condition ofthe world today and prays fervently, then people can be helped without infringement upon their free will."

CHAPTERONE Prayer ls a Powerful Weapon Much Prayer ls Needed I n the past, in arder to do something, a worldly person would think. If he were a spiritual person, he would think and pray. In our time, even "spiritual" people not only don't pray, they don't even think.ln fact, this frequently involves serious matters, and al! they do is practice on people. In al! circumstances, befare we act we should say, "Have 1 thought about this? Have 1 prayed about this?" When someone acts without thinking and without praying, he acts satanically. And you often see many Christians acting in a way that doesn't allow God to intervene. They imagine that they can manage everything by themselves. Whereas even an unbeliever may say an occasional "God will provide", the "believer" won't. Someone may have strong objections, for example, to the carnival and try to attack it directly, whereas he can pray and God will pour down such hail that it will scatter everyone and cancel every activity. Or, let's say, sorne people criticize a bishop and the others make an appeal to the Council of State. But they don 't even stop there. They go on to have demonstrations, disturbances, l

334 SI'! RITUAL WEAPO"'S articles in the newspapers... An endless array of human efforts that leave no room for God to act. They don 't resort to prayer, for God to respond through prayer. With humility and prayer all the seemingly unsolvable problems can be corrected.

Today we live in apocalyptic times. It doesn't take a prophet to understand this. Things are moving fast. What awaits us, we don 't know. That a crisis is coming is clear from the current situation. And this is one more reason why we must now rely more on prayer and fight evil with prayer. This is the only solution. We must appeal to God to have merey on His creation - although we are unworthy of His pity. There will be no end to the problems. Everyone will do whatever comes into his head.

What Saint Cosmas said will take place: One will walk for hours to find a human being; and those who survive will be eating with golden spoons. Of course, sorne think: "Since the prophecies will be fulfilled anyway, what's the use of prayer?" God sees events unfolding in this manncr, but we still pray for sorne evil to be less harmful and to be limited in scope. That's why it is written in the Gospel that for the elect s sake those days sha/1 be shortened. 1 For example, in a war a miracle takes place through the power of prayer, more people are saved, there are fewer casualties, in which case people are helped spiritually, believe and are transformed in the good sense of the term.

The situation is serious. It is a miracle that the world is still here and hasn't been blown to pieces. May God put His hand upon the world and save it, for the whole world depends on three or four individuals. The fate of l. Cf. Mt 24:22; Mk 13:20. 335 the world is in the hands of a few madmen. The proverb says, The horses kick and the chickens die. That's how it is. When the great countries fight each other, it's the small countries that suffer injustices. The large nations kick each other and the small ones are destroyed. Much, much prayer is needed to enlighten the great leaders of the world, for, ifthey want to, they can destroy the world.
God can give them a little enlightenment very easily. If God enlightens, someone can give an order and everything changes.

Petitions in Prayer - Geronda, with your blessing, tell usa few things for which we should be particular! y praying. - First of all, we should beseech God that our prayer will draw those who are now living and those who will live in the future to devotion to God. In my prayer l say, Extend your merey on those who know Thee and then l add, and on those who do not know Thee. I even say, Lord, save the impious. (Of course the Church has properly arranged to say, Lord, save the pious, for the impious may be cursing the pious for praying for them.) When again the priest says, For those who have asked us, unworthy though we be, to pray for them, l like to add and for those who have not asked us to pray for them. Because we should pray for those who ask us and for those who don't ask us, for those we know and for strangers, too. There are so many thousands of people who have greater need and more serious problems than those who have asked us to pray for them. We should also pray for those who have suffered injustices, that
336 SPIRITUAL WEAPO:-.S justice may prevail, that grace may be given to those in prison that their sufferings may be transformed into opportunities for spiritual help.

When I put wood on the tire, I praise God and say, "My God, provide warmth for those who don't have it." When again burn the letters people send me - I read them and burn them because they contain prívate material and personal confessions - I say, "May God burn up all their ftaws; may He help them lead a spirituallife and sanctify them." I also make ita habit to ask the Saints to protect the people who bear their name, and the Communion of Saints to protect!hose without a patron Saint. - Geronda, which is better, to ask for God's merey in general, orto refer to specific and concrete requests in accordance with As k, and it shal/ be given you.' - You should pray in general and say, "LordJesus Christ, have merey on!hose who are suffering in body and in spirit." This prayer includes those who have fallen asleep in the Lord. When someone you know comes to mind, say a prayer for him, Lord Jesus Christ, have merey on your servant so-and-so, and then move on to a prayer for everyone, Lord Jesus Christ, have merey upon the whole world. You may, of course, bring to mind someone who is in need and pray a little for that person, but then you should say, Lord Jesus Christ have merey upon us and be pained for everyone so the!rain (i.e. the prayer) doesn't lea ve with only one passenger. We shouldn 't gel caught up with one person and then be unable to help either ourselves or others with our prayer. For instance, when you pray for a cancer patient, pray for all cancer patients, and

2. Mt 7:7. ~rayer ls a Powerful ~~apon 337 add a prayer for all those who ha ve already fallen asleep in the Lord. Or when you encounter sorne unfortunate person, let your mind and heart reach out immediately to all the unfortunate people and pray for them. As a child remember a beggar who died outside a Turkish home, ten meters away from our home. His name was Petras. The Turkish woman found him in the morning lying outside her home and nudged him to get up; she then realized he had died. I still remember him in my prayer. How many such "Petras" are there in the world!

When we think of specific matters and see that our fellow human beings are suffering, our heart is moved, and we benefit from this. Thus, our pained heart can move from specific matters to the more general and provide greater help through prayer of the heart. lt is good for a monk to divide his prayer into three parts: for himself, for the whole world and for!hose who have fallen asleep in the Lord. Even though this division seems fair, nevertheless, he is still showing more care for himself, because he is only one person, whereas all the other living and departed are in the billions. - Geronda, while rendering service to the monastery, I usually only pray for myself. - This isn't right. When you pray for yourself in your cell and pray for yourself again outside of your cell, how can your heart be moved and stirred? When there is!ove, pain and sacrifice, a higher concern enters and sweetens the heart. That way no one forgets to say the Jesus Prayer while working. This way the whole world benefits, and the person who is praying also benefits and experiences spiritual joy. Do your obediences and pray, Lord Jesus Christ have merey on us, so everybody may benefit. Have

338 SPIRITUAL WE:APOI'<S merey on us covers everyone, even those five or six world leadershowever many they may beu pon whom the fate of the whole world depends. Work and pray for those who are also working. Such prayer covers those who are working physically and those who are working spiritually.

There are many who are working and they're working hard. Sorne are working for the good of the Church and of the State. Others are working to bring about harm; they stay awake searching for ways to destroy the world.

Still others band together to find ways to prevent the previous ones from doing evil. Others again work day and night to find solutions to the general problems of the world. Pray then that God may enlighten even the evildoers to do less evil, or prevent them altogether from doing anything evil. Pray that God enlightens the good people to help the world. Pray for those who want to work but cannot because they are sick. Pray for thc healthy who need work but cannot find any. Think ofthe various cases that may exist and pray for their solution.

When the mind goes out to all of these people, the heart is stirred by the pain and suffering and then the Prayer becomes prayer of the heart. There are so many people who have a need in the course of the da y. There should be no waste oftime. From the person who doesn't have a great need for prayerregardless of whether he thinks he does - one can move on to think of all those whose need is greater. Thus help comes to the one who thought he had a need, because he became the cause for the other person to pray for many.

Prayer -~~-~ Powerful Wea_pon 339 Help Through Prayer - Geronda, Saint James says, The supplication of a righteous man availeth much in its working. 3 What is meant by Availeth much in its working? - That it is al so necessary for someone seeking the prayer of a righteous man to want to be helped, to be saved, and to make an effort too. In other words, someone who wants to benefit from the prayer of a righteous man must ha ve a good disposition. Prayer from the heart is heard; but the recipient must also be receptive. Otherwise, the person who is praying must have the sanctity of Saint Paisios the Great to be able to bring the other person out of hel\. 4 So, first pray for those who wish to be saved.

When 1 ask God in prayer to help in various cases, 1 say, "My God, may Your help be obvious, so the people may benefit spiritually too; if it cannot be visible, don't help us." Many people are not at all aware what storms God has saved us from, nor do they think of it, so as to praise God. That is why we should ask Christ, Panaghia, the Saints to help people, but to make their help perceptible so the people can benefit spiritually from it.
Let's say someone is in danger of falling from sorne scaffolding and God provides that he be caught, whereas

4. In the lit'e of Saint Paisios the Great the following event is related: A negligent novice fell in sorne sin out of disobedience and later died, before he could repent. God revealed to his Spiritual Father, afler persistent prayers, that thc soul of the novice will be tormented in Hades until the Day of Judgment when he would receive the appropriate recompense. The Fathcr then went to Hosios Paisios and asked him to pray for thc salvation of his novice. Afler thc fervent prayer of Hosios Paisios, Christ delivered the soul of the unrepentant novice from Hades.
340 SPIRITUAL WEAPONS ordinarily he would not ha ve been caught, and be saved.

Or he falls somewhere and is not seriously injured. Or again he survives an accident. We should pray that this person understands that God helped and saved him and thus be helped spiritually by recognizing this fact. Someone fell from a bridge and was saved. I told him, "Did you go down to measure the depth?" lt is the Saints who hold us in their hands. A young manto whom I had given a small cross to wear was speeding with his motorcycle; he passed in front of a taxi, flipped around and continued on the road, without being harmed at all. Many are spared from harm, but few are!hose who realize it and mend their ways. - If I pray for someone but he doesn 't ask for help, will he be helped? - That depends on many things. First of all, if he was of good will and was not helped for sorne reason, we can say that he is entitled to sorne divine help. Even if others don 't pray for him, God willlater pro vide for him to be helped. But if he himself asks for help, God will intervene earlier and he will be helped earlier; otherwise he will be helped later. With prayer we give God the right to intervene. Normally, when a person asks for help from his heart and says humbly, "Father, pray for me too," it breaks your heart and you can't forget him. Whereas if he says flippantly, "Pray, pray, pul! sorne beads through your fingers forme," it is like saying, "See you!". How can you remember him?

When prayer is offered from the heart, with pain, for the problems of the world, all those who are asking God for help at that time will immediately receive it. I havc many concrete examples. Many years ago, a man who Prayer ls a -~-~~·crful Weapon 341 was in profound despair wandered across the border, into the Iron Curtain. There, mistaking him for a spy, they seized him, tormented him and put him in prison. While in prison, he prayed. A monk known tome learned ofhis situation and prayed for him. "My God, I beseech You, free him. This is easy for You todo." After the imprisonment, they called him to explain himself. After this that man found himself on Greek territory. When the Greeks questioned him, he said, "They beat me, they imprisoned me, and asked me to explain myself. After that l don'! remember anything. I neither know nor understand how l found myself at the guard-house on the Greek border." This is the power of prayer. Prayer moves God. lt's good for our "wireless" to work constantly, to embrace al! the people who are seeking help. In the army, in the Signals Corps, we kept!rack of the foreign stations, but we also helped our own. We served as a transmitter for our stations that were too far away from headquarters to be heard clearly; we too k their signals and transmitted them. There were always two ofus working together two shifts. For if only one man was working, he would have had to interrupt and would not be able to help!hose seeking help, and his signals would not have reached their destination. Such a station, when working constantly, can help those whose signals don't reach their unit. By the same token, one who is bold befare God and prays for others serves asan intermediary between!hose seeking help and God. But what happens when one asks for help and the other has his station closed down, that is, is not praying?

342 SPIRITUAL WEAI'ONS The Quality of Prayer Counts - Geronda, when we have a serious request, should our prayer be accompanied by fasting? - That goes without saying; it's a mus t. F asting and ascetic struggle are prerequisites for prayer. But for prayer to be proper and effective one must have compassion for others. For it is a rule ofmany Christians in our time not to want to have any worries. Even retired people who sit around al! day don't want to approach an abandoned child, for this requires work and effort, which will disturb their serenity. They will eat, have their coffee and take a walk; they may even go to see a patient in a hospital that has nurses and is well-organized, because this is easy. In other words, again for their own pleasure; for they ease their conscience with the thought that they have done their duty. I have pressured many people to help sorne poor abandoned children. My pleas have fallen on deaf ears.

Somewhere on the Holy Mountain they had a prayer service for the drought and, instead of rain, a fire broke out! A prayer service for a drought is not like going for a walk. We need to be troubled and pained over the prevailing situation. Can there be any temptation or difficult situation remaining once the monks have prayed fervently from the heart? In spite ofthe difficult years we are going through, I see in the monasteries a prevailing spirit... to be happy! Here the world is buming, and we're trying to be happy. People ask us to keep a vigil, for example, to help sorne sick person, and we chant the festive psalm Thou apenes! Thine hand, they are jilled ~~-~y~r~s a Powerful Weapon 343 with good, 5 and we feel joyful. We are passing our time pleasantly, but, in the meantime, the patient is dying. And they say, "We are keeping a vi gil for the sic k person." What kind ofvigil is that? You are entertaining yourself; that is spiritual entertainment. Sometimes, even when the priest says, Let us pray for those who are sick, we don 't pray for the sic k. We could be of more positive help to the sick if we devoted sorne time to the Jesus Prayer with the komboschoini. I am not saying, of course, that we should do away with the festive vigils prescribed by the liturgical calendar of the Church, but in such cases we can devote a little time to pray fervently one or two rounds with the komboschoini, Lord Jesus Christ have merey upon your servan t.

The main thing is the quality of our prayer. Prayer must be from the heart; it must be said with pain and compassion. What counts more for God is not the quantity of prayer but the quality. The prayer practised in the monasteries has quantity, but this is not enough; it must also have quality. So many hours of prayer by so many people, if it were prayer of the heart, would ha ve changed the world. For this reason, the goal should be for the Services to be so celebrated that they are prayer ofthe heart. Prayer ofthe heart not only helps others but also ourselves, because it helps us to acquire inner goodness. When we put ourselves in others' shoes, lo ve then comes naturally, as does compassion, humility, and our gratitude to God with continuous praise, and then prayer for our fellow human beings is acceptable to God and helpful to them.

344 SPIRITUAL WEAPONS "But They Constrained Him" 6 - Geronda, my heart remains cold during prayer. - This is because the mind doesn't send a telegram to the heart. After all, one must do a great deal of work in prayer; one cannot attain from one moment to the next a steadfast condition of prayer, so that the mind doesn 't wander at all. Patience is needed. You see, someone knocks on the door, knocks again, waits, and then the door opens. You seem to want to knock once and enter directly. lt doesn 't work this way. When you begin to say the Jesus Prayer, don't you have a desire for it? - I feel the need for help, because 1 feel weak. - Well, that's where one starts from. In prayer, we must be persistent. The Evangelist says, But they constrained him, about the two disciples who met Jesus on the road to Emmaus 7 Christ remained with them, because they hada relationship with Him, and they were entitled. They had humility, simplicity, goodness and even boldness, in the good sense, all the prerequisites, and for this reason Christ stayed with them.

We must pray with faith for every matter and we must have patience, and God will speak. Because when man prays with faith, God is in a sense obligated by virtue of our faith to fulfil our request. For this reason, when wc ask for something from God, let us never be in doubt and we shall be heard. The Lord said, Havefaith and doubt not. 8 God knows when to give us what we ask for, so we won't be harmed spiritually. Sometimes we ask God
8. Cf. Mt 21:2 l. )'rayer Is a Powerful Weapon 345 for something, but we have no patience and are troubled.

But, since we have an Almighty God Who has so much!ove that He nurtures us with His Body and Blood, we have no business being troubled. Sometimes we don't leave a matter in God's hands but instead try to deal with it in a human way. When we ask God for something but have doubts and act by human means to achieve what is really difficult, without waiting for God 's answer to our request, it is like making a petition to God, our King, and then withdrawing it justas He stretches out His hand to act on our behalf. We beseech Him again, but again we doubt and are troubled and repeat the same thing.

Thus, we are tormented continuously. We behave like the person who petitions the Ministry, and, after a while, changes his mind and withdraws his petition. He changes his mind again and resubmits it; after a while, he again withdraws it. The petition, however, has to stand in order to wait its tum.

Prayer With Pain - Geronda, how do you pray about a certain matter~ - The main thing is to feel compassion. 1f one is not compassionate, he may sit for hours with the komboschoini, and his prayer will have no result whatsoever.
If there is pain in his heart about what he is praying for, even with a single sigh he will have prayed from the heart.

Many, who when asked to pray have no time, will sigh for the other's problem and that in itself is a prayer. I 'm not saying prayer needn't be done, but, ifthere is no time, a sigh for the pain of another is a prayer from the heart; it equals hours of prayer. You read, for example, a letter,

346 SPIRITUAL WEAPONS you realize there is a problem, you sigh over it and then you pray. This is very important. Befare you even pickup the telephone receiver, even befare you call, God hears you! And how the other person leams ofthis! You should see how those possessed by demons realize when 1 am praying for them, and cry out wherever they may be.

Real prayer begins from pain; it is not a pleasant experience, a "nirvana". What kind of pain is it? One is troubled in the good sense of the term. He feels pain, he groans, he suffers when praying for anything whatsoever.

Do you know what it means to suffer? Yes, he suffers, because he is participating in everyone's pain or in the pain of one particular person. This participation, this pain, is rewarded by God with divine exultation. Of course, he doesn 't ask for this exultation, but it comes as a consequence, because he participates in the pain of others. - How will he begin? - Let's say he leams of an accident and sighs, and, at that moment, God gives him divine consolation for his compassionate sigh. He sees someone who is hurting, and immediately feels compassion, and is thus consoled by God with di vine consolation; he doesn 't remain with that initial bittemess over the accident. The other person is helped through his prayer. Or he may think to himself, "God has given us so many blessings; what have I done for God?" I was impressed by what one soul said to me, "The Angels cover their faces when the Mystery of the Holy Eucharist is being celebrated, while we commune with the Body and Blood ofChrist. What have I done for Christ?" He suffers in a good sense. - Geronda, how does one understand that someone has been helped by his prayer?

Prayer Is a Po_werful Weapo_n 347 - He realizes it from the divine consolation which he feels inside after praying from the heart in pain. But first, you must make the other person's pain your own and then pray from the heart. Love is a di vine attribute and informs the other person. Even in hospitals, when the doctors and nurses feel genuine compassion for their patients, this is the most effective medicine of all the medications given to them. The patients feel they are being cared for with!ove and have a sense of certainty, security, and consolation.

Yo u don 't need to say much to someone who is suffering or try to instruct him. He understands that you feel his pain and care about him, and he is helped by this. Feeling his pain is everything. lf we feel compassion for others, we forget ourselves and our problems.

Divine Consolation - Geronda, when 1 feel pain for others, I become anxious and cannot pray. - This anxiety shows that there is a human element in you. The more 1 am pained by people's sufferings, the more I pray and rejoice spiritually at telling Christ everything and having Him take care of it. And I notice that as time passes and physical courage diminishes, spiritual courage increases because!ove, sacrifice, and compassion for others provide great spiritual strength. And I must admit that this evening during your vi gil, 9 1 wasn 't really up to it, but I received renewed strength from the

9. The Elder is referring to the vigil of the 9th to the 10th of November 1993. In spite ofhis grcat suffering from cancer, he stood, holding on toa chair, and gave his blessing to approximately thirty thousand people who passed by that da y. l

348 SPIRITUAL WEAPOI\S pain of others. And I was able to stand through the night until the Divine Liturgy by seeing all the people. Even later, standing in the Church, I didn't feel tired because 1 felt compassion for those people and this strengthened me. You, too, must pray and rejoice, because Christ will take care of everything.

There is no grief in the spiritual encounter. With those people now I went through so much pain! I didn't pass over their problems lightly. I was pained by them, I sighed over them, but with every sigh 1 tumed the matter over to God and in the pain I felt for the other person God provided consolation. That is to say, divine consolation carne with the spiritual approach, because the pain which carries with it hope in God also has divine consolation.

Otherwise how is one to endure! How could I possibly get through all the things that 1 hear? Yes, 1 feel the pain, but I also think of the divine reward in those who are suffering. We are in God 's hands. Since there is di vine justice, divine reward, nothing is lost. The more one is tormented, the more he will be rewarded. Although God sees so much suffering upon the earth, even things which we cannot imagine, He never falters. "You have suffered more?" He asks, "I will provide you with more in the other life", and He rejoices. Otherwise, how could He endure so much injustice, so much evil that exists? But He keeps in mind the reward ofthose who suffer and, in a manner of speaking, endures that great pain. We don't see the glory to be received by the other person and so we feel compassion for him, and for this God rewards us with divine consolation. - Geronda, won 't this worry exhaust us? -No. Someone who faces every problem spiritually is P_r_ayer _Is a P?werf!JI Weapon 349 not exhausted. At first, he is grieved, embittered, when he hears that someone is suffering, but then di vine consolation comes as a reward and the human organism is not destroyed. While bittemess from worldly worry brings gastro-intestinal illnesses, and so on, spiritual compassion does not harm our organism because it has the divine balsam.

The Danger lJ{ lnsensitivity - Geronda, sometimes, lay people ask me to pray for sorne problem they have. 1 do it, but without feeling any pain for them. - Here there are two cases: the first is dangerous, the second has a spiritual resolution. When the monk forgets his family and doesn't think of others either, that is, doesn 't pray for the world, this is very bad. We come to the monastery, we abandon our family, and we end up forgetting not only our family but even more so the others. We see things spiritually, but we don't share in others' pain spiritually. We don 't make spiritual progress, to be able to feel their problems, and there is a danger of becoming insensitive. Indifference creeps in and the heart becomes like stone. In the second case one feels pain for everyone, but also finds consolation in knowing that the sufferer will ha ve a reward from God for being a martyr.

With this thought, one feels a deep certainty and has an inner joy. In this case the heart isn't stony, but di vine. lfmonks and nuns aren't careful, their hearts can become very hard. Lay people see accidents, the suffering of others, and are pained. We don't see this suffering and may pray only for ourselves. That is, if we don 't work on

350 SPIRITUAL WF:APONS ourselves to learn to feel the misfortunes of others, so that we will be able to pray for them from the heart, we may become hard-hearted. We may reach the point ofwanting to make ourselves comfortable and having a heart stony from indifference, a condition that is contrary to the Gospel. The monk must care for, be pained over, and in general pray for the people. This is not a distraction, but, on the contrary, he himself is helped by the prayer and so are the others. - Geronda, while 1 see my own many faults, 1 pray more for others. Would it perhaps be better not to pray for others and to pray only for myself? -Out of humility? lf, indeed, it is out of humility, then very humbly say to God, "My God, wretch that l am, 1 am unworthy to be heard by Yo u. But is it fair for others to suffer because of me? For, if I had a good spiritual state, an authentic boldness before You, You would have listened to my prayer and helped them. I, too, am to blamc for the sufferings of others. But now why should they suffer because of me? Please help them." In other words, it depends on how you see yourself with regard to other people. You feel unworthy, but it happens that you see someone who is suffering, and so you worry about them, you are pained and you pray.

When, for example, I see a blind man, 1 feel guilty because, if 1 was in a proper spiritual state, 1 would be able to heal him. God has given us the potential to become saints, todo miracles, as He Himself di d. We acknowledgc our great or small spiritual illness and humbly ask for the physical health of our fellow human being, as being responsible for his illness. For if we had spiritual health, he would have been healed long ago and not suffered.

Pr~_ycr ls a Powerful Wcap<!n 351 When we realize that we are responsible for the condition of all the people and say, Lord, Jesus Christ, have merey upon us, everyone benefits. We should grieve over our own many faults, but also seek God's merey. Naturally, when one reaches a good spiritual state, he doesn 't ask anything for himself. 1 see that we often take the Jesus Prayer the wrong way by saying, Lord, Jesus Christ, have merey on me and, presumably out ofhumility, avoid saying, Have merey on us, thus not praying for others, but only for ourselves.

This is one reason why lay people sometimes misunderstand us monks and say that we are egotists who only seek to save ourselves. We use the singular, Have merey on me to avoid falling into pride. The prayer of a humble person, who believes himself to be the worst of al!, is of greater worth than an all-night vi gil done by a proud person. When we pray with pride, we are fooling ourselves.

A State ofAlert Do not forget that we are going through difficult times and much prayer is needed. You must remember the great need people have for prayer today, and the great expectation God has of us to pray. Pray for the general outrageous condition of the whole world that Christ may have pity on His creations, for they are heading for destruction. Pray for His divine intervention in our outrageous time, for the people are heading into a general confusion, into madness and an impasse.
God has called us to pray for the people, who have so many problems. The poor people don't even have time to cross themselves. lfwe who are monks and nuns

352 SPIRITUAL WEAPONS don't pray, then who will? The soldier in wartime is on alert, always ready with his shoes on. The monk must also be on alert. How 1 would have loved to be a Maccabee, '" to withdraw to the mountains and pray constan ti y for the world.

We must help everyone with our prayer and not Jet the devil ha ve his way with them. The devil has acquired rights. Not because God has given him permission, but because He doesn 't want to vio late man's free will. This is why we can help through prayer. When one is pained over the prevailing condition of the world and prays, then people can be helped, without violating free will. If yo u continue with the Grace of God a little further, we can begin to make sorne progress on the issue of prayer, to put into effect a certain arder, to be a radar station of prayer, for the urgency of the times requires it. We must organize a prayer taskforce. You must wage war with the komboschoini. Pray with pain in your heart for the world.

Do you know what great power such prayer has? 1 am deeply hurt to see monks who act in human ways and not with prayer through God in matters which are difficult to achieve by human means. God can put everything in arder. When one does good spiritual work, then, through prayer alone, one can build monasteries, equip them with all the necessary things and help the wholc world. There is no need to even work, as long as one 1O. The name Maccabees was given to Judah, leader of the Jewisb rebellion of 166 BC against Antiochos IV Epiphanes, ruler of the kingdom of Seleucids. The title, according to the more probable opinion, refers to the one who hammered the enemy. The same name was given also to thc successors of Judah. The Maccabees distinguishcd themselves in the struggles for thc traditional faith and for political freedom of the Jewish people. (Sec 1, 2 and 3 Maccabees.) A monk must try not to worry over every littlc difficulty, whether it is personal or affects a fellow human being, or even the general state of society, but should instead resort to prayer and send, through God, many di vine powers. You say, "I have sinned." He then stops and gives you blessings. In other words, the unruly child comes to his senses and is tormented by bis conscience. Then the Father caresses His child with!ove and comforts it. Because the Israelites had strayed from God's commandments, they lived in exile for seventy-five years. Finally, when they had repented, Cyrus the king appeared, who one may dare to say proved himself better than the sons of Israel, who had polluted the sacred traditions oftheir worship of the true God. God changed Cyrus' mind and made him believe in the God of Heaven. Consequently, he set the Israelites free to retum to their homeland and provided money and supplies to rebuild the Temple and the walls of Jerusalem. Cyrus showed such kindness and devotion that not even the Israelites themselves had shown. 10 And all this happened because the people repented and changed their way of life. 11 You see how much repentance can help in getting rid of evil!

Read all the books of Maccabees; they are very powerful. The king gave an order that the elephants be prepared to trample and kili the Israelites in a bloody ceremony. Five hundred elephants were given handfuls of frankincense and plenty of strong wine to enrage them 1O. Cf. 1 Esd 1: 1 f. (LXX).

The Deeper \1eaning of Life 395 for the cruel deed. Everybody was waiting for the king to appear, so the ceremony could begin. But the king had forgotten the order he had given. The man in charge ofthe elephants went to inform the king, who had yet to show up. "Come, O king, we await you. Everything is ready." But the king replied angrily, "Who told you to prepare for such a thing?" And this did not happen once but three times. 12 It was no small matter for the king to forget each time the order he had given repeatedly. And not only this, but in the end he completely changed his attitude toward the Jewish people, who with tears had prayed to their merciful God and Father to avert the danger facing them.

True repentance is the key. - Geronda, do the peace organizations established by various countries help in bringing peace to the world? - It depends. There are sorne who begin with good intentions. But when all sorts of unrelated people - sorcerers, fire-worshippers, cultic religious types of all persuasions - enter into the mix and attempt to work together, how can there be a common purpose ora common ground for peace? God forgive me, but such things are the devil's rags. Can peace be achieved through sinful association?

How can there be peace, when people are not reconciled with God? Only when man is reconciled with God can inward and outward peace come. But for us to be reconciled with God, we must first attain awareness, we must repent, we must live in accordance with God's commandments, and then the Grace and peace of God will enter into us, enabling us to also help create peace around us.
